title: Garlic and lemon infusion for colds
subtitle: A warming pungent brew for the first days of a cold
excerpt: Fresh garlic cloves simmered in water with lemon juice, sipped at the start of a cold. Heat tames the raw-garlic sharpness and keeps the working sulphur compounds in the cup. One of the simplest cold-season kitchen brews.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), garlic entry. Garlic as an antimicrobial, chest-clearing, decongestant herb. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), simmered garlic for colds and chest complaints. WHO Monograph on Allium sativum (1999), antimicrobial mechanisms documented.

content.text: Three crushed cloves of garlic simmered in water for ten minutes, strained, and drunk hot with lemon. The heat takes the raw sharp edge off so the brew is easier to drink, while keeping the working sulphur compounds in the cup. The heated

by-products are less sharp than the raw form but still hold their antimicrobial bite. A kitchen tradition for the first shivery days of a cold. The quickest garlic remedy of the bunch: no overnight steeping, no tincture-making, just a cup of garlic broth ready in 20 minutes.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Blood thinners: garlic can thin the blood a little. One to three cups a day is unlikely to matter for most people. If you take warfarin, aspirin, or other blood thinners, discuss regular daily use with your prescriber.

Pregnancy: cooking garlic in pregnancy is well established as safe. One to two cups a day during a cold sits close to a cooking range. Regular use across pregnancy is best discussed with a midwife.

Seek medical care for a cold that worsens after the first three days, a high fever lasting more than two days, or trouble breathing.
attrs.title: Medical notes

content.text: Crush or roughly chop {{garlic}}. Place in the small saucepan with {{water}}. Bring to a gentle simmer and simmer covered for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a mug. Allow to cool slightly. Stir in honey and lemon juice if using. Drink slowly while still hot.

One cup, up to three times daily during the acute phase of a cold. Make fresh for each dose. The traditional course is three to five days, the duration of the acute cold phase. If the cold is not improving after five days, see your doctor.
When NOT to use this remedy

Talk to the prescriber if you take blood thinners. Seek medical care for a cold that worsens after early improvement, for a high fever lasting more than two days, for breathlessness, or for green or bloody mucus.
